text: The surgeon uses an Enseal device to coagulate the round ligament approximately three centimeters lateral to the uterus, which measures sixteen centimeters in length and contains two fibroids located on the anterior and posterior walls.

The surgeon uses two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port. In the left hand, a bipolar Maryland forceps is used, while the right hand holds the Enseal device. The round ligament is coagulated and remnants are cut. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is slowly opened, revealing the fallopian tube.

The surgeon uses the Enseal device to dissect and cut the remaining portion of the mesosalpinx. The ovarian ligament is separated, and the ovary is preserved. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is opened.

The surgeon moves to the left side of the surgical field, where the sigmoid colon is visible on the extreme left. The round ligament on the left side is dissected four centimeters from the uterus. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is separated towards the cervix and uterovesical fold. The fallopian tube is visible, and the surgeon uses bipolar cautery to coagulate it at two points before cutting between them with scissors. The ovarian ligament is also visible.

The surgeon opens the anterior peritoneum at the UV fold, separating the bladder from the retroverted uterus using a uterine manipulator to push the colpotomizer. The posterior peritoneum is also opened above the utero sacral ligament, revealing the uterine artery knuckle. The peritoneum is dissected downward to facilitate suturing or further dissection using the Enseal device.

The surgeon separates both the anterior and posterior peritoneum at the level of the uterus. Using the Enseal device, the entire vascular bundle, including the uterine artery, is sealed and cut with multiple applications for security. A clamp is applied near the cervix, and the left side dissection of the vascular pedicle is completed. The Enseal's articulation allows precise positioning near the cervix, facilitating the coagulation and cutting of the uterine pedicle. Additional clamps are used for the veins and remaining parametrium tissue dissection.

The surgeon secures the vessels on either side and uses a uterine manipulator with a colpotomizer to begin colpotomy. A harmonic scalpel is used to cut around the colpotomizer cup, opening the lateral fornix and revealing the plastic tip of the colpotomizer. The anterior colpotomy is completed, followed by the posterior and right lateral attachments of the vaginal vault. The cervix is held with a tenaculum, and the uterus is pulled through the vagina. Due to the size of the uterus, bisection and morcellation are performed using vaginal speculums to protect the bladder and rectum during removal.

The surgeon takes a bite through the anterior and posterior layers of the vagina, including the vaginal fascia and epithelium, using an extracorporeal knot to ligate the tissue. The angles of the vagina are sutured with Vicryl or PDS sutures, approximating the uterosacral ligament with the vaginal angles. The suture is fed through to prevent shearing, and an extracorporeal square knot is tied, locking the suture multiple times to ensure the vaginal vault is securely closed.
